skill.name: video-extend
skill.description: Extend or continue an existing video clip on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes to Google Veo 3-1's `extend-video` and `fast/extend-video` endpoints — pick the source video plus a prompt describing what should happen next, and the model produces a clip that continues the original with consistent motion, lighting, and subject identity. Use when the user has a short Veo clip and wants it longer, or wants a chained narrative built shot-by-shot from a single seed clip. Triggers on "extend video", "continue video", "longer video", "video extend", "make this clip longer", "Veo extend", "chain video shots", "video continuation", or any explicit ask to take an existing video and add more frames after it.
skill.summary: Extend or continue an existing video clip on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes to Google Veo 3-1's `extend-video` and `fast/extend-video` endpoints for consistent motion, lighting, and subject identity.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: All 78 static analysis findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The static analyzer misinterpreted Markdown backtick formatting (inline code) as Ruby shell backtick execution in this Markdown documentation file. All URL references are legitimate documentation links to RunComfy service, model catalog, and related skills. The single filesystem reference documents token storage for user awareness. The skill is a legitimate video extension tool that uses the runcomfy CLI and Google Veo 3-1 models. No malicious intent, prompt injection attempts, or obfuscated behavior detected. Skill uses external commands, network access, and filesystem access as documented and expected for this type of tool.

low_findings.title: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 44 instances of backtick characters in SKILL.md as Ruby shell backtick execution. The file is a Markdown document where backticks are used for inline code formatting (e.g., `runcomfy`, `extend-video`). These are not code execution constructs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Backticks in a .md file are Markdown formatting, not Ruby code. The file has no Ruby code, no .rb extension, and no require/inherit Ruby constructs.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs flagged as hardcoded network endpoints
low_findings.description: 24 URL references were flagged as hardcoded network endpoints. These are legitimate documentation links to the RunComfy homepage, model catalog, API documentation, and related skill pages. They serve as user reference material, not executable network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to runcomfy.com, skills.sh, or docs.runcomfy.com domains which are the legitimate service provider and documentation sites. UTM parameters confirm marketing attribution, not malicious tracking.

low_findings.title: Token storage documentation flagged as filesystem access
low_findings.description: Line 140 references ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json in the Security & Privacy section to document where the runcomfy login command stores authentication tokens. This is transparency documentation, not actual file system access code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reference appears in a dedicated Security & Privacy section that explains token storage to the user. The skill accesses RUNCOMFY_TOKEN env var, but documents the credential file path for user awareness.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm false positive
low_findings.description: Lines flagged for weak crypto (lines 5, 9, 82, 120) contain only YAML frontmatter license field and plain English text about prompting tips and exit codes. No cryptographic algorithms exist in the file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No base64-encoded strings, cipher references, or cryptographic function calls exist in the SKILL.md file. The analyzer appears to have false-triggered on unrelated content.

low_findings.title: Usage guidance misidentified as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Lines 53 and 58 contain usage guidance comparing the standard Veo 3-1 Extend and Fast Extend model tiers. These advise when to use each endpoint for cost control, not reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines discuss model endpoint selection based on quality vs cost trade-offs. No system commands, environment queries, or diagnostic operations are present.

content.user_title: Extend and Continue AI-Generated Video Clips
content.value_statement: Video clips from AI generation tools often end too soon, forcing creators to start over for longer scenes. This skill extends any existing Veo-generated clip by chaining continuation prompts through Google Veo 3-1, keeping subject identity, lighting, and motion consistent across each extension.
actual_capabilities: Extend an existing Veo video clip by adding frames through a text continuation prompt
Chain multiple extend calls to build longer narratives from a single seed clip
Switch between standard extend and fast extend endpoints for cost control
Provide prompting guidance for consistent camera motion across extensions
Route video continuation requests to the appropriate Veo 3-1 model endpoint
Support one-action-per-beat prompting for best video quality results
limitations: Only works with Veo 3-1 model endpoints through RunComfy, not other video services
Each extend call adds limited duration; long videos require multiple chained calls
Subject identity may drift after 3 to 4 chained extensions without re-anchoring
Requires a source video URL from a prior Veo generation
Requires RunComfy CLI to be installed and an active RunComfy account with credits

use_cases.title: Short clip to full scene
use_cases.description: A filmmaker generates an 8-second establishing shot and needs a 16-second scene. This skill extends the clip with a matching continuation prompt, preserving lighting and camera motion.

use_cases.title: Shot-by-shot narrative building
use_cases.description: A storyteller creates scenes one at a time, chaining each extended output into the next call to build a multi-shot sequence with consistent character placement.

use_cases.title: Cost-controlled video iteration
use_cases.description: A producer drafts multiple extend variations using the fast endpoint before locking the final sequence on the full-quality extend endpoint.
use_cases.target_user: Production editor working within budget constraints

prompt_templates.title: Basic video extension
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend the video at [source video URL]. Continue the scene naturally.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user extending a single clip

prompt_templates.title: Extend with camera direction
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend the video at [source video URL]. Camera continues pushing in slowly. The character looks down at the object on the table.
prompt_templates.scenario: User adding specific camera and subject direction

prompt_templates.title: Chain multiple extends into a story
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend the video at [seed clip URL] with prompt: 'camera cuts to medium close-up; character reacts to off-screen sound'. Then extend the output again with: 'character turns and walks toward window'. Use fast extend for drafts.
prompt_templates.scenario: User building a multi-shot narrative sequence

prompt_templates.title: Cost-optimized multi-draft workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate 3 draft extensions of this video using the fast endpoint: [URL]. Draft A: slow dolly in. Draft B: character exits frame left. Draft C: camera pans to reveal new element. Then render the winning draft with the full quality extend endpoint.
prompt_templates.scenario: Production workflow with iteration and final rendering

output_examples.input: Extend the video at https://cdn.example.com/sunset-clip.mp4. Continue the camera slowly panning right as the sun sets further.
output: The skill routes the request to the Veo 3-1 Extend endpoint
A new video file is generated continuing the sunset scene
The original 8-second clip becomes a 16-second clip with consistent lighting
The extended clip is saved to the specified output directory

output_examples.input: Take this 8-second establishing shot and chain three story beats: character enters from the right, character looks at object on table, character picks up the object.
output: The skill creates three sequential extend calls using the fast endpoint
Each extend feeds the previous output as input for identity consistency
Three new video segments are generated forming a continuous narrative
The user can review each segment and re-render at full quality

best_practices: Keep extend prompts focused on one action beat per call for the best results
Use the fast extend endpoint for draft iterations and full quality for final renders
Monitor identity drift across chained extends and re-anchor with an image-to-video call when needed
anti_patterns: Describing the entire scene again in the extend prompt instead of only what happens next
Chaining more than 4 extends without re-anchoring, which causes identity drift
Using the full quality extend endpoint for early draft iterations, wasting budget

faq.question: What video formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill accepts video URLs from prior Veo 3-1 generations. The RunComfy CLI handles format conversion automatically.

faq.question: How long can a single extend call make my video?
faq.answer: Each extend call approximately doubles the clip duration. Typical Veo clips start at around 8 seconds.

faq.question: Can I extend videos from other AI video tools?
faq.answer: This skill is designed for Veo 3-1 generated clips through RunComfy. Other video tools use different extension methods.

faq.question: Does the skill require paid accounts?
faq.answer: Yes. You need a RunComfy account with available credits to use the video extension endpoints.

faq.question: How do I install the RunComfy CLI?
faq.answer: Install with npm: npm i -g @runcomfy/cli. Then sign in with runcomfy login or set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable.

faq.question: What happens if my video prompt causes identity drift?
faq.answer: Identity naturally shifts across generations. For long narratives, re-anchor with an image-to-video call using a frame from the last good output.
